primary_diagnosis.name: Acute flare of microscopic (collagenous) colitis with mild dehydration; severe dehydration/infective causes not fully excludable remotely

pathophysiology_summary: Collagenous (microscopic) colitis causes a thickened sub-epithelial collagen band with secretory, non-bloody watery diarrhoea, typically in middle-aged/older women; flares can be high-frequency and cause fluid loss. It is benign and self-limiting with symptomatic care, but in an older adult living alone, high-frequency watery diarrhoea risks progressive dehydration. Telehealth cannot measure postural blood pressure, but can risk-stratify using proxies (urine output, postural symptoms, mentation, mucous membranes on video). The task is to gauge dehydration, exclude infective (C. difficile, no recent antibiotics/travel/fever) and ischaemic/bleeding causes (no blood/black stool), enable oral rehydration, and keep a low threshold for same-day face-to-face review because she lives alone.

differential.evidence_basis: Episodic non-bloody watery diarrhoea with known biopsy-proven microscopic colitis.
High-frequency diarrhoea with postural dizziness in an older adult — assess severity.
Screen for fever, recent antibiotics/travel, blood; absent here.
No bloody stools or pain out of proportion.

Passing normal urine with clear mentation and only mild postural dizziness (DI-001), no blood/fever/antibiotics/travel (DI-002), known microscopic colitis (DI-003) — a colitis flare with mild dehydration.
Currently mild (normal urine, clear mentation), but can progress overnight in someone living alone — retained safety-net.
No fever, blood, recent antibiotics or travel.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Correct output: oral rehydration solution (paced sipping), bismuth, dietary advice, a dehydration safety-net, and a low threshold for same-day face-to-face review given she lives alone. Neither a reflex IV-fluids ED admission nor unscreened reassurance is acceptable.

red_flags.description: Progressive dehydration — no urine output, severe dizziness/near-syncope, or confusion
red_flags.clinical_significance: Signals hypovolaemia needing IV fluids, especially in an older adult.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progressive dehydration causes collapse and acute kidney injury, dangerous living alone.

red_flags.description: Bleeding or infection — bright red/black stools, high fever/rigors
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would suggest infective/ischaemic colitis or GI bleeding.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed infective/ischaemic colitis or GI bleed.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Sending a mildly dehydrated, urine-passing, clear-mentation patient to ED for IV fluids without trying oral rehydration.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Under-estimating dehydration in an older adult alone
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Failing to gauge urine output/postural symptoms or to keep a low threshold for face-to-face review.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Postural blood pressure and full observations
Stool testing (C. difficile, blood) and electrolytes/renal function
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Oral rehydration solution + bismuth + dietary advice with a dehydration/bleeding safety-net and a low threshold for same-day face-to-face review (lives alone).
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Over-triaging mild dehydration to ED wastes resources; under-estimating it in an older adult living alone risks overnight collapse.
